paragraphs.body: Afghanistan is a landlocked country in Central Asia, bordered by Pakistan, Iran, Turkmenistan, Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, and China. Afghanistan’s geography reflects its resilience and history.

Afghanistan’s landscape is a striking contrast of rugged mountains, vast deserts, and fertile valleys. The towering peaks of the Hindu Kush dominate the horizon, while the deep valleys and plains offer a glimpse of the country’s hidden beauty, shaped by centuries of history and struggle.
Cultural Heritage Me: hey, how do you feel about being Afghan? Do you feel proud?

Friend #1: “Being Afghan has come with so many challenges, and I know the struggles aren’t over. For our ancestors, being Afghan meant honor, bravery, and a strong spirit. For me, being Afghan is all about remembering my father’s journey and staying connected to my roots. It doesn’t matter what others think about our identity. As an Afghan girl who’s living through the worst times—the Taliban era—being Afghan means pride. I was born Afghan, and I’ll always be Afghan.”

Friend #2: “Being Afghan is complicated for me. On one hand, when I think of Afghanistan’s rich history, I feel proud. But on the other hand, the current state of my country leaves me feeling drained and hopeless about my identity. No one can truly hate their birthplace, but right now, I can’t help but feel discouraged. Afghanistan is such a big part of who I am, but sometimes I wonder how different my life would be if I weren’t Afghan—maybe I would have had better opportunities and a brighter future. I wish my Afghan identity had come with better circumstances. Despite everything, I am still proud to be Afghan, but I can’t ignore the challenges we face.”

Exile I remember the nights when my mom couldn’t sleep. The house would be silent except for her steady but burdened breathing. She would sit there, scrolling through pictures of my siblings and their kids, her fingers gently brushing the screen as if trying to touch them through the pixels. I’d watch her, feeling the weight of her sadness fill the room. Her tears would fall silently, not only because she missed them but also because she couldn’t protect them from the dangers back home. I couldn’t help but feel the same ache. Every time I looked at their pictures, I’d feel this deep emptiness—the kind that comes from being so far away. I couldn’t hug them or feel the warmth of their presence. I would sit alone, staring at their faces on the screen, wishing the distance between us could somehow disappear. But it didn’t. The more I stared, the more I realized how unreachable they were and how this digital connection was all we had. I would hear my mom’s voice quiver when she spoke to them, and I’d feel my own heart break because I knew it wasn’t enough. We were both in this painful limbo—connected yet distant, loving yet unable to touch. The silence after every call was louder than words; it was the absence of everything we once took for granted. Perhaps one of the reasons I hold my Afghan identity so close is that it ties me to the very essence of who I am: my family. The struggle for something as basic as an education is a constant reminder of Afghan women’s strength and resilience. The Afghan daughter, brave and strong for her dreams, fights lifelong. Though in ruins, she builds gracefully; like Malalai, she guards her place. In chain, she lives, yet her heart is free; through all her sorrows, kind she’ll be. Poem Rohullah Nikpai is Afghanistan’s first Olympic medalist. As a refugee in Iran with no formal training, he clung to hope and belief in himself. His journey from exile to global success shows how resilience can turn challenges into triumphs.

Identity Everything I am today is rooted in my Afghan heritage. Afghanistan’s history taught me the significance of treating others as I wish to be treated. Its hardships instilled in me empathy and resilience. The struggles my people endured have shaped my strength and sense of accountability. My country’s history is intricately woven into my values, and it is through my Afghan identity that I have become who I am today.

annotations.text: Everything I am today is rooted in my Afghan heritage. Afghanistan’s history taught me the significance of treating others as I wish to be treated. Its hardships instilled in me empathy and resilience. The struggles my people endured have shaped my strength and sense of accountability. My country’s history is intricately woven into my values, and it is through my Afghan identity that I have become who I am today.
annotations.comment: This is my favorite passage in this essay. I am really impressed by the way you examine your identity. Where as so many of us would say something like "I play tennis" or "I love Jazz" when considering identity, you seem to be saying, "I am the mountains, I am my people's struggle."

paragraphs.body: Kaleidoscope Me: Hey, do you feel proud of being Afghan?

Friend #3: “Being Afghan is an integral part of my identity, and it fills me with a deep sense of pride … My roots and traditions are a source of strength and resilience. Living in Afghanistan, I am constantly aware of the socio-political challenges that surround us. Yet, I remain undeterred in my commitment to my country and my people. I believe that, even in the face of adversity, there lies an opportunity to grow and contribute positively to society. I am determined to make the most of the opportunities available to me, striving for a future where I can not only achieve my own aspirations but also pave the way for other young generations in Afghanistan … While the circumstances in Afghanistan may be challenging, my pride in being Afghan remains unwavering. I embrace my culture, and I am committed to being a force for positive change. My journey as an Afghan girl is one of strength, hope, and determination.”

Legacy of Resilience: “Sarzameen Man My Homeland: Once again, I realized that I don’t merely carry the title of Afghan—I carry the spirit, hope, and dreams of my people. My identity is a tapestry woven from both the beauty and pain of my homeland.

annotations.comment: I would love to see you explore this further. I know that resilience is important to this piece, but I'm not sure how this title relates to the passage or the photo below.

Hello, Thank you for sharing this very personal essay with me. The central focusing idea of this piece is clearly a consideration of a cultural and national identity through your own experiences and observations. This consideration is supported by your big ideas of history, family, and cultural trends. You do a great job of focusing on these themes throughout the piece. The words that stand out to me the most in this piece are family, friend, and history. Obviously, the word Afghan is the white hot center of the piece, but I think that this essay would not be as powerful as it is if you weren’t doing such a good job writing about the history of your country and the ways in which that history affects your family and friends to this day. It’s your focus on these notions that makes this a very personal essay, rather than an encyclopedia entry. In fact, it’s that personal examination of your identity that I admire most about this piece. You approach this writing with a great deal of pride, but, also, an awareness of the struggle that your country has faced and the resilience which has grown out of that struggle. All this really is impressive. The primary audience of this piece, interestingly to me, seems to be you, yourself. You are weighing your own identity, your place in the world, your nation’s place in the world. It’s this consideration of your nation’s place in the world that brings about your much broader audience which is, it seems to me, Americans—specifically, Americans who might not have a personal connection with Afghanistan (or that part of the world at all). The purpose of this writing goes beyond a mere reflection of personal identity and serves to introduce people to your country through the experiences and observations of someone for whom the landscape, history, and geography of the country is central to their identity. What I learned from reading this essay is that you place a great deal of value in your nation. It really is impressive how, in an examination of your own identity, you describe the mountains and valleys, your Olympic hero, your mother viewing pictures. These are some of the things that define who you are, what you value, what makes you human. It truly was a pleasure reading this piece. Thanks,
